subject: AP Computer Science Principles
theme: All data in a computer is represented as bits, and understanding how data is stored, compressed, and analyzed is essential to working with digital systems.
core_concepts: All data in computers — text, images, audio, video — is ultimately stored as sequences of bits (0s and 1s). With $n$ bits, you can represent $2^n$ distinct values.
Analog signals are continuous; digital signals are discrete. Analog-to-digital conversion samples an analog signal at intervals — higher sampling rate = higher fidelity.
Lossless compression reduces file size without losing any data (the original can be perfectly recovered); lossy compression permanently removes data to achieve smaller sizes.
Metadata is data about data — a photo's metadata might include its location, date, and camera settings, even if that information is not visible in the image itself.
Overflow errors occur when a result is too large for the allocated number of bits — the computer wraps around or produces an incorrect value.
Correlation means two variables change together; causation means one variable directly causes changes in the other. Correlation alone never proves causation.
Data cleaning (removing inaccurate records), filtering (selecting a subset), and transformation (applying operations) are essential steps before analyzing large datasets.

key_terms.definition: The smallest unit of digital data — a single 0 or 1.
A group of 8 bits, capable of representing 256 distinct values.
A base-2 number system using only 0 and 1 — the fundamental language of computers.
A continuous signal that can take any value in a range, like sound waves or light intensity.
A discrete signal represented as a sequence of 0s and 1s.
Measuring an analog signal at regular intervals to produce a digital approximation.
Compression that reduces file size without permanently removing any data — original is fully recoverable.
Compression that permanently removes some data to reduce file size — original cannot be perfectly recovered.
Data that describes other data — such as a file's size, creation date, or author.
An error when a value exceeds what can be stored in the fixed number of bits, producing an incorrect result.
A statistical relationship where two variables change together, without implying one causes the other.
Correcting or removing inaccurate, incomplete, or inconsistent records from a dataset.
Selecting a subset of data that meets specified criteria for focused analysis.
A base-16 number system using digits 0–9 and letters A–F, often used as shorthand for binary.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests whether a given scenario is lossless or lossy compression, and whether a relationship between two variables is correlation or causation — always ask 'does A directly cause B?' before claiming causation.
